Re: 
Request for reconsideration of
llritten Advice (6ovt. Code Section 83I14(b))
Your file t{0. A-90-499
oear ilr. Hallabrin and Is. Stecher:
As the Actlng CitJ AttorneJ for the City of South San Francisco (effective
January 2, 1991) it is respectfu'l'lJ requested on behalf of Councilmember ,rohn
Penna that there be a reconsideration of the xritten adyice letter dated
october 12, 1990, based upon the Request for yritten Adyice letter dated July
27, 1990, of the former City Attorney (a copJ of each letter ls attached for
easy reference).
It is acknorledged by Counci lneflber Penna that he read and made no objection
to the content of the July 27, 1990 'letter before it vas mailed to the FPPC.
Hoxever, after receipt and reyiey of the Adyice Letter dated october 12, 1990,
vith reflectlon upon holr certain phrases were used together xith incomplete
staten€nts in the July 27, 1990 Request Letter, Councilmember Penna is of the
bel'lef that a clarification of facts may resu'lt in a dlfferent conclusion so
that he may part'lcipate in decisions for lrhich he was elected.
Councllme[ber Penna bel'leves he has no present or future conflict of interest
regardlng decisions on the Shearxater project, but he has and Yill faithfully
abide by the advice of the FPPC.
The essential polnt is that Counci lmember Penna does not intend to pursue
buslness opportunit'les relating to the Shearrater proiect, [hich 'ls one of
three separate redeyelopment project areas ir the City.

(4r5) 877-8515


Fair Po'li t i cal Practice Cormission
Page 2
January 14, 199I
HODIFIED BACKGROUND
The City of South San Francisco is a community of approximately 52,000
persons. There are 1,500 acres in the City limits zoned for coflmercial and
industrial use, out of a total of 9.53 square miles. ApproximatelJ one fourth
of the City is zoned comercial and industrial and three-fourths is zoned
residentia'1.
Prior to 1985, the City Council, acting as the City Redeveloprnent Agency,
formed two present'ly active redevelopment project areas, namelJ the Do'/intown
project area, and the Gateway project area.
In 1986, at the behest of a developer, the City Council and Redevelopment
AgencJ (same members as City Counci'l) created a third redevelopment project
known as Shearwater for a site encompassing 54 acres of dry'land and 133 acres
of rater. A master planned mixed use development is proposed.
The Shearvrater project area is under one ownership together with six acres
(called the 'GSA" parcel) owned by the City. A tentative map for 12 lots on
the non-city owned property was approved in 1986, but a lawsuit fited in 1986
by the City of San Francisco challenged the tentative map and the EIR, In
March 1989, the Court of Appeal remanded to the trial court to reconsider the
residential portion of the Plan by reason of CEQA factors aod proximitJ to the
San Francisco Airport, The'litigation de'layed progress on the project. A
supp'lemental EIR was prepared in 1990.
The City owned GSA parcel 'is considered to be integral to the development.
Pursuant to an outstanding agreement with the developer, the CitJ anticipates
se'l ling or leasing the GSA parcel to the deyeloper. Details of the
transaction are be'ing negotiated by the City l4anager and the developer,
subject to direction and u'ltirEte approval by the City Council, The
transaction was expected to conclude by fall of 1990. The developer has had
financial problems with backers from Japan and due to the litigation delay.
As of this date there is no assurances of concluding any transaction.
The individual components of the project, such as the approval of the final
subdivision map and at'l of the approvals for the buildings (retail, office,
hote'|, possible condominiums) as vrell as the specific design of a marina, will
be subject to various discretionary approval processes as the proiect is
constructed in phases. In addition, the developer has requested the City to
establ ish an assessment d'istrict and a 'landscape and I iqhting maintenance
district for var,ious financing purposes, The tentative map expiration date 'is
December 31, 1991, which reflects a stay for the'litigation period.
In November 1989, John Penna vas e'lected to the City Council. l"lr. Penna is a
real estate broker and has orned Penna Realty, a local real estate company'
for the past 26 years. He operates as a one-man office without any agents.
His only office ls in the Downtown redeveloFnent area. over 601 of


Fair Pol itical Practice Comission
Page 3
January 14, 1991
l4r, Penna's business is done in South San Francisco. The business inc'ludes
nanagenent of properties within the Dot{ntovn redeve] opment area, real estate
sales of residential, coDnercial and industrial property, and real estate
apprai s i ng and consulting.
In December 1989 and January 1990, the Shearwater project began to move
forward again after the I itigation hiatus. Councilmember Penna participated
in sone discussions and fair'ly routine development actions. He received
briefings on ownership structure and an appraisal report pertaining to the
C i ty's GSA parcel,
TRI Realtors is the agent for marketing and leasing the Shearwater site. Any
broker, houever, can bring a client directly to the developer and receive a
full commission for the leasing or sale of space in the project.
During llarch 1990, Counci'lmember Penna approached the Shearwater developer,
who he has known since 1984, to advise the developer that he was going to the
upcoming International Association of Realtors (FIABCI) conference in
Acapulco, Mexico. He offered to present the Shearwater Project hotel site
offering at the FIABCI conference at his olrn expense. Pursuant to an
authorizing 'letter, obtained by Councilmember Penna from Shearwater at the
request of FIABCI, he made a presentation on the Shearwater Project hotel site
to approximate'ly 1,000 brokers attending the conference, using materials
supplied by the developer. If a broker came forward as a result of that
presentation and went through Councilmember Penna to present an offer,
Councilmember Penna could have received a comission. The authority expired
at the conclusion of the FIABCI conference in l4ay 1990. To date, no broker
has come forvard and none are contemplated,
In April, 1990, Councilmember Penna advised the former City Attorney of his
authority and intention to make a presentation on behalf of Shearwater at the
FIABCI conference on the hotel site. In the course of his d'isc'losure he
explained hor he conducted his business. The former City Attorney assumed
from his explanation and 'incorrectly presented in the JulJ 27, 1990 Request
for Adv'ice Letter that Councilmember Penna had been working with a broker
prior to the conference on the Shearwater hotel site. Councilmember Penna's
sole activity has been the presentation of the hotei site at the FIABCI
conference in May 1990, and his authority was then concluded. Counci lmember
Penna did not receive any monetary compensation and has had no financial gain.
In ltlay the former City AttorneJ advised Councilmember Penna that due to the
then current negotiations for the hotel and the pending presentation in l'lexico
that he should refrain from participating in several Shearwater matters coming
before the Agency/Council, and he followed that advice. Since the approva'ls
being sought re'late to the financial feasibility of the proiect and since the
discussion concerning the terms of sale of the City-owner property have a
financia'l impact on the proiect, to the extent Councilmember Penna Yas
pursuing a bi/siness re'lationship rith the developelit ras the oPinion of the
iormer iity AttorneJ, after seeking oral advice from the FPPC, that it did not


Fa'ir Pol'i ti ca I Practice Comnission
Page 4
January 14, 1991
seem appropriate for Councilmember Penna to
bus i nes s.
part i ci pate in Agency/Council
As indicated previously, to date there have not been any leads, listings,
pending or comp'leted sales resulting in a financial return to Councilmember
Penna, Consequently, he believes he has a right to participate in future
Agency/Counci'l discussions and votes concerning various aspects of the
project, including the lease or sale of public'land and the
formation/imp'lementation of assessment and maintenance districts. He also
be'lieves that as a realtor he has a right to participate in transactions
throughout the City vhen he has no conflict of interest. Councilmember Penna
acknowledges that as an active rea'l estate broker a business opportunity could
occur in the Shearwater project just as lrith'in any other area or neighborhood
of the CitJ, but that he has no present or future intention of pursuing
bus'iness opportunities in the Shearwater project. In short, the FIABCI
presentation was a one-shot effort and it has been concluded without success,
PERSONAL ANALYSIS
Councilmember Penna contends that in view of his office location, property
oy/nership, and propertJ management business all being in the Downtown
redevelopment area and having no property or business interests in the
SheariJater project, the dec'isions of qq{19}_!ql9S. v. !-9u9}_!-9@qilJ.
Develo0nent Com. 196 Cal.App,3d 983 (1987) and Ulll! v, Morrow 70 Cal.App.3d
817 (1977), and the FPPC opinion of In re oq'lesby, are not in point since each
involve an indiv'idual with property ownership and interests within the subject
redeve'lopment project areas. Councilnember Penna holds no property or
business interests in the Shearwater project area.
area is separate from the trJo other redevelopnent
in an action for one having no effect on the other
The Shearrater project
project areas resulting
project areas.
Councilmember Penna acknorledges that as an active real estate broker his door
is open to receive business and that he invites business generally through
advertising, but he questions the use of the phrase "that he may pursue
opportunit'ies related to the Shear}/ater project,,.." ("Question' on page I,
and page 6, line 7 of advice letter dated 0ctober 12, 1990). To "pursue"
denotes 'to find or employ measures to obtain or accomplish." Rather than
'pursue" perhaps a better term is that as a real estate broker Councilnember
Penna is "accessible",'capable of being reached", for business opportunities
general ly.
Finally, Councilmember Penna contends that if there is a foreseeability under
facts 'piesented above that a decision by him on the Shearwater proiect will
resu'lt in a materia'l financial effect or gain on his business, then such is
tantamount to hold'ing that every active real estate broker or agent e'lected to
the council has a foreseeable conflict of interest on all land use rnatters
since there is a possibi'lity that any given council or aqency action may


Po'lltical Practi ve Commi ssi on
January 14, 1991
Page 5
enhance propertJ values or result in greater numbers of propertJ transactions
anyrhere in the City, and thus produce financia'l gain to counci'l elected
realtors by being accessible to possible, but not nec€ssarily, prospective
opportunities.
OUESTIOI{S
1. If Councilmember Penna participates now as a member of the
Agency/Council in discussions and actions concerning the Shearwater project,
and six months, or eighteen months, from now as the project is underray he has
a client rho xishes to engage in 'lease or purchase of a portion of the
project, is there a conflict and rhen does it arise?
2. If, as a member of the Agency/Council, Counci'lmember Penna
participates in decisions that enable the project to go forriard, does that
preclude him from being involved in the project in his capacity as a realtor?
3. Ihen a project, such as Shearxater, has numerous integrated parts and
will require nultiple approvals for different phases over a period of time, 'is
there a conflict of interest if a councilmember actiyely seeks to transact
private business during the pendency of a phase of the project or the entire
project?
4. lf a City Council is engaging in negotiation and sale or lease
transactions *ith a deve'loper, is there a conflict of interest if a
councilmember participates in those activities and subsequently engages in
prlvate business transactions, particu'larly if the counci'lmember has no
specific deal pending at the time of the negotiat'ion and sa'le or lease?
5. As an additional, but more specific questionr may Councilmember Penna
be present at either open or closed sessions of the Agency/Council when
information regardJng Shearxater or the GSA Parcel sites are discussed?
DONALD R. HAILE
Acti ng City Attorney
DRH/m
cc: Blanche Breeze, FPPC Counsel
Counci lmember Penna
City llanager JesG Armas
submi tted,

sections.question:
1. If Councilmember Penna participates now as a member of the Agency/Council in discussions and actions concerning the Shearwater project, and six months, or eighteen months, from now as the project is underway he has a client who wishes to engage in lease or purchase of a portion of the project, is there a conflict and when does it arise?

2. If, as a member of the Agency/Council, Councilmember Penna participates in decisions that enable the project to go forward, does that preclude him from being involved in the project in his capacity as a realtor?

3. When a project, such as Shearwater, has numerous integrated parts and will require multiple approvals for different phases over a period of time, is there [document truncated]

sections.question_synthetic: Councilmember Penna seeks reconsideration of a prior FPPC advice letter regarding whether his participation in Shearwater project decisions creates a conflict of interest given his role as a real estate broker who offered the hotel site at a conference and could potentially receive commissions from future transactions related to the project.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document is an incoming request letter seeking reconsideration of prior advice, not an FPPC response letter. The questions section appears to be truncated at the end of the provided document. This is correspondence FROM the City Attorney TO the FPPC requesting reconsideration, not a response FROM the FPPC.

embedding.summary: This is a request for reconsideration of FPPC written advice (File No. A-90-499) dated October 12, 1990, submitted by the Acting City Attorney on behalf of Councilmember John Penna of South San Francisco regarding potential conflicts of interest related to his participation in decisions concerning the Shearwater redevelopment project while serving as an active real estate broker.
